---
title: "CMake — Getting Started"
subtitle: "Generate a Visual Studio or Xcode project from a simple C++ Hello World"
author: "ESTG IPLeiria"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-copy: true
    code-fold: false
    smooth-scroll: true
    embed-resources: true
    css: |
      
      :root {
        --accent: #e04c3a;
        --accent2: #2563eb;
        --bg-dark: #1a1d23;
        --bg-code: #f4f6f9;
        --border: #e2e8f0;
      }

      body {
        font-family: 'Sora', sans-serif;
        font-weight: 300;
        color: #1a1d23;
        font-size: 1.05rem;
        line-height: 1.75;
      }

      h1, h2, h3 {
        font-weight: 700;
        letter-spacing: -0.02em;
      }

      h1 { color: var(--accent); }
      h2 { border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); padding-left: 0.6rem; margin-top: 2.5rem; }
      h3 { color: var(--accent2); }

      code {
        font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
        font-size: 0.88em;
        background: var(--bg-code);
        padding: 0.1em 0.4em;
        border-radius: 4px;
        color: var(--accent);
      }

      pre code {
        color: inherit;
        background: none;
        padding: 0;
      }

      .callout {
        border-radius: 8px;
      }

      .platform-badge {
        display: inline-block;
        padding: 2px 10px;
        border-radius: 99px;
        font-size: 0.78em;
        font-weight: 600;
        margin-left: 6px;
        vertical-align: middle;
      }

      .badge-win  { background: #dbeafe; color: #1d4ed8; }
      .badge-mac  { background: #fce7f3; color: #9d174d; }
      .badge-both { background: #dcfce7; color: #166534; }

      table {
        width: 100%;
        border-collapse: collapse;
        font-size: 0.95rem;
      }

      th {
        background: var(--bg-dark);
        color: #fff;
        padding: 0.6rem 1rem;
        text-align: left;
      }

      td { padding: 0.5rem 1rem; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border); }
      tr:hover td { background: var(--bg-code); }
---

```{=html}
<div style="background:linear-gradient(135deg,#1a1d23 60%,#2d3340);color:#fff;padding:2.5rem 2rem 2rem;border-radius:12px;margin-bottom:2rem;">
  <div style="font-size:0.8rem;letter-spacing:0.15em;text-transform:uppercase;color:#e04c3a;font-weight:600;margin-bottom:0.5rem;">Game Engines · Advanced Programming</div>
  <h1 style="color:#fff;font-size:2rem;margin:0 0 0.5rem;border:none;padding:0;">Introduction to CMake</h1>
  <p style="color:#94a3b8;margin:0;font-size:1rem;">Build system generator — one project description, multiple IDE outputs</p>
</div>
```

## What is CMake?

**CMake** is a *build system generator*. You describe your project once in a `CMakeLists.txt` file, and CMake generates the native build files for your platform:

| Platform | Generated output |
|----------|-----------------|
| Windows  | Visual Studio `.sln` / `.vcxproj` |
| macOS    | Xcode `.xcodeproj` |
| Linux    | Makefile / Ninja |

> **Key idea:** you never write a `.sln` or `.xcodeproj` by hand — CMake does it for you.

---

## Prerequisites

### Install CMake

Download the installer from **[cmake.org/download](https://cmake.org/download/)** and follow the steps for your OS.

::: {.callout-tip}
During installation on Windows, select **"Add CMake to the system PATH"** so you can run it from any terminal.
:::

Verify the installation:

```bash
cmake --version
```

You should see something like `cmake version 3.28.x`.

### Install a compiler / IDE

<span class="platform-badge badge-win">Windows</span> Install **Visual Studio 2022** (Community edition is free). Select the **"Desktop development with C++"** workload during setup.

<span class="platform-badge badge-mac">macOS</span> Install **Xcode** from the App Store, then accept the license and install Command Line Tools:

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

---

## Project Structure

Create the following folder structure anywhere on your machine:

```
hello_cmake/
├── CMakeLists.txt
└── src/
    └── main.cpp
```

You can create it from the terminal:

```bash
mkdir hello_cmake
mkdir hello_cmake/src
```

---

## Write the Source Files

### `src/main.cpp`

```cpp
#include <iostream>

int main() {
    std::cout << "Hello, CMake!\n";
    return 0;
}
```

### `CMakeLists.txt`

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.16)

# Project name and language
project(HelloCMake CXX)

# Require C++17
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 17)
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)

# Define the executable and its source files
add_executable(HelloCMake src/main.cpp)
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Line by line:**

- `cmake_minimum_required` — the oldest CMake version that works with this file.  
- `project(HelloCMake CXX)` — names the project and declares C++ as the language.  
- `set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 17)` — enforces C++17.  
- `add_executable(...)` — tells CMake to build an executable named `HelloCMake` from the listed source files.
:::

---

## Generate the IDE Project

CMake separates **source** from **build**. Always generate into a separate `build/` folder — this keeps your source tree clean.

### Windows — Visual Studio {.unnumbered}

<span class="platform-badge badge-win">Windows</span>

Open a terminal (Command Prompt or PowerShell) inside `hello_cmake/`:

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -G "Visual Studio 17 2022"
```

Then open the generated solution:

```bash
start build\HelloCMake.sln
```

Or navigate to `hello_cmake/build/` in Explorer and double-click `HelloCMake.sln`.

::: {.callout-tip}
If you have **Visual Studio 2019**, use `-G "Visual Studio 16 2019"` instead.
:::

### macOS — Xcode {.unnumbered}

<span class="platform-badge badge-mac">macOS</span>

Open Terminal inside `hello_cmake/`:

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -G Xcode
```

Then open the project:

```bash
open build/HelloCMake.xcodeproj
```

---

## Build and Run

### Inside the IDE

<span class="platform-badge badge-win">Windows</span> In Visual Studio, press **Ctrl+F5** (Run without debugging) or set `HelloCMake` as the **Startup Project** and press **F5**.

<span class="platform-badge badge-mac">macOS</span> In Xcode, select the `HelloCMake` scheme at the top and press **⌘R**.

### From the terminal (any platform) {.unnumbered}

```bash
cmake --build build
```

Then run the resulting binary:

```bash
# Windows
build\Debug\HelloCMake.exe

# macOS / Linux
./build/Debug/HelloCMake
```

Expected output:

```
Hello, CMake!
```

---

## Common CMake Flags Reference

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `-S <path>` | Source directory (where `CMakeLists.txt` lives) |
| `-B <path>` | Build directory (output folder) |
| `-G "<generator>"` | IDE / build system to generate for |
| `--build <path>` | Build without opening the IDE |
| `--config Release` | Build in Release mode (optimised) |
| `-DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release` | Set build type for single-config generators |

---

## Quick Troubleshooting

::: {.callout-warning}
**"cmake is not recognized"** — CMake was not added to `PATH`. Re-run the installer and check the option, or add it manually.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
**"No CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER could be found"** — Your compiler is not installed or not detected. On Windows, install Visual Studio with the C++ workload. On macOS, run `xcode-select --install`.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
**Visual Studio shows a blank startup project** — Right-click `HelloCMake` in the Solution Explorer → **Set as Startup Project**.
:::

---

## Summary

```{=html}
<div style="background:#f4f6f9;border-left:4px solid #e04c3a;padding:1.2rem 1.5rem;border-radius:0 8px 8px 0;font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace;font-size:0.9rem;line-height:2;">
  <span style="color:#6b7280;"># 1. Create files</span><br>
  CMakeLists.txt + src/main.cpp<br><br>
  <span style="color:#6b7280;"># 2. Generate (Windows)</span><br>
  cmake -S . -B build -G "Visual Studio 17 2022"<br><br>
  <span style="color:#6b7280;"># 2. Generate (macOS)</span><br>
  cmake -S . -B build -G Xcode<br><br>
  <span style="color:#6b7280;"># 3. Build & run</span><br>
  cmake --build build
</div>
```

That's it — three steps from source to running executable, on any IDE. As your projects grow, you'll add more `add_executable` or `add_library` calls and link targets together, but the workflow stays exactly the same.
